symbols: bread — the body of Christ broken for the remission of sins
chalice of wine — the blood of the New Covenant poured out for many
Christ at center — the host and sacrificial Lamb presiding over his own memorial feast
red pitcher in foreground — blood of the covenant, the outpouring of sacrificial life
richly decorated interior — the sanctity of the Upper Room as the first church
Judas turning away — the betrayer who ate bread with Christ yet sold him for silver
description: Thirteen men are gathered around a rectangular table covered with a white cloth in an ornate interior. The central figure wears a red cloak and holds a golden chalice in one hand and bread in the other. The twelve surrounding figures display varied gestures of surprise, prayer, and conversation. A large red ceramic pitcher sits on the floor in the lower left. The background features marble columns, a decorative green tapestry, and arched windows revealing a distant landscape.
theological_description: This depiction of the Last Supper captures the institution of the Eucharist as recorded in the Synoptic Gospels. Christ, centrally enthroned among his apostles, holds the bread and cup — the elements he designated as his body and blood of the New Covenant (Matthew 26:26-28). The varied reactions of the disciples likely reflect the dual moment when Jesus both announces his betrayal ('one of you will betray me,' Matthew 26:21) and institutes the sacred meal. The Passover context is critical: as the original Passover lamb's blood delivered Israel from the angel of death (Exodus 12:13), so Christ the true Paschal Lamb offers his blood for the deliverance of all humanity (1 Corinthians 5:7). Paul received this tradition and transmitted it as of first importance — 'Do this in remembrance of me' (1 Corinthians 11:23-25). The ornate setting elevates the Upper Room to a proto-sanctuary, the birthplace of Christian liturgy where the sacrifice of the cross is anticipated and made present in sign.

scripture_refs.reason: Matthew's account of the institution of the Eucharist at the Last Supper, with Jesus breaking bread and sharing the cup
Luke's narrative of the Last Supper including the words of institution over bread and wine
Jesus announces his betrayal at the supper, reflected in the apostles' varied reactions
Paul's transmission of the Last Supper tradition — 'do this in remembrance of me'
The original Passover meal typologically fulfilled in Christ's institution of the new covenant meal
